metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the regver staleness check — e.g., a temporary feature flag (`REBAR_SKIP_REGVER_STALENESS=1`), a grace-period window, or a documented one-command revert procedure — so the breaking invalidation can be reversed without a full code rollback.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a behavioral breaking change — all pre-existing attestations (lacking a `regver` line) will be treated as stale immediately after deployment, triggering mandatory re-review for every project. The plan acknowledges this with 'expand-contract, never a crash' but provides no rollback/back-out mechanism if the re-review storm is unexpectedly large or if a deployment needs to be reverted.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'compute_validity gains a regver staleness check'; Remediation preamble: 'An attestation with NO regver line (pre-this-change) → treated as stale → re-review (expand-contract, never a crash)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single environment-variable escape hatch (e.g. `REBAR_REGVER_GRACE=1`) that causes `compute_validity` to treat a missing regver as matching (rather than stale) for a defined transition window; document the flag and its removal timeline in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the overlay disable contract (e.g., a `"disabled": true` routing-entry field, or a top-level `"disable": [id]` list in the overlay) and how `effective_criteria` / `route_criteria` skip disabled built-ins — this upstream mechanism must exist before `coverage.disabled_builtins` can ever be non-empty.
findings.finding: The plan's `disabled_builtins` feature presupposes a built-in-disable mechanism in the overlay that does not exist in the current codebase. Story ef7e (which the plan cites as the source: 'from the overlay disabling a built-in via ef7e\'s merge') explicitly deferred disable support to a 'later story'. The current `_validate_routing_entry` only validates exec/block_threshold/default_posture; `effective_criteria` always includes all CANONICAL_LLM members regardless of any overlay entry. Without a disable mechanism upstream (a way to mark a built-in as disabled in the overlay AND have `effective_criteria`/`route_criteria` skip it), `coverage.disabled_builtins` will always be empty — AC3 becomes vacuously satisfied and the signed manifest never reflects reduced coverage as intended.
findings.location: What section ('A disabled built-in is recorded in a disabled_builtins: [ids] field'); AC3 ('A disabled built-in appears in coverage.disabled_builtins in the signed manifest'); remediation AC2 ('build_manifest emits a deterministic disabled_builtins: [ids] line when the verdict coverage has disabled built-ins')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `disable` list to the overlay schema (e.g., `{"disable": ["G5"]}`) processed in `effective_criteria` / `effective_routing` to exclude the named built-ins, and propagate skipped ids into `coverage.disabled_builtins` in `assemble_verdict`. Update `_validate_routing_entry` or the overlay loader to recognise and validate `disable` entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define exactly which overlay field/value marks a built-in as 'disabled' (e.g., a `disabled: true` key in the routing entry or `block_threshold: null`) and add it to the `_validate_routing_entry` schema spec so `disabled_builtins` population is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `coverage.disabled_builtins` (AC3, What, remediation AC) and `manifest_disabled_builtins` parser, but never defines what constitutes a 'disabled' built-in in the overlay schema. The existing `_validate_routing_entry` validates only `exec`, `block_threshold`, and `default_posture` — there is no `disabled: true` sentinel or equivalent field. An implementer cannot populate `disabled_builtins` from the overlay without knowing which overlay key/value signals 'disabled'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `disabled: true` boolean field to the routing-entry schema in `_validate_routing_entry`, treat its presence on a built-in id as 'disabled', and exclude it from the active run while recording its id in `coverage['disabled_builtins']`. Reference this field explicitly in the plan's What/AC3 sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or add the upstream mechanism that populates `coverage.disabled_builtins` (either a `disabled: true` routing field in the overlay merge path in `registry.py`, or a dedicated removal from `effective_criteria`), and name it explicitly in the Scope/What section — AC3 cannot be delivered until this producer exists.
findings.finding: The plan's `disabled_builtins` feature (AC3, remediation AC2, and the 'What' section) depends on an overlay built-in-disable capability that does not exist in the current codebase: `registry.py:283-284` implements only re-tune (`merged[cid] = {**merged[cid], **entry}`), and story ef7e's own ticket comment explicitly deferred disable to a later story. There is no `disabled: true` field, no removal-from-active-set logic, and no producer that populates `coverage.disabled_builtins` — making `build_manifest`'s new `disabled_builtins:` line unfeasible as described until the upstream disable mechanism is implemented.
findings.location: What section ('disabled_builtins … folded into the signed manifest … from the overlay disabling a built-in via ef7e's merge'); AC3; remediation AC2 ('build_manifest emits a deterministic disabled_builtins: [ids] line when the verdict coverage has disabled built-ins')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `disabled: true` sentinel to the overlay routing schema and handle it in `_effective_routing_cached`: when `entry.get('disabled') is True` for a built-in id, record it in a module-level set rather than merging into `merged`, and exclude it from `effective_criteria`. Then populate `coverage['disabled_builtins']` in the orchestrator's verdict assembly from that set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `refresh_attestation` reads `disabled_builtins` from the prior manifest (via the new `manifest_disabled_builtins` parser) and forwards it into the reconstructed coverage dict so the drift-refresh path preserves reduced-coverage state.
findings.finding: The plan adds `disabled_builtins` to `build_manifest` / `coverage` but provides no protocol for the `refresh_attestation` drift-refresh path. Tool-verified: `refresh_attestation` (attest.py lines 392-422) reconstructs the coverage dict solely from `blocking:` and `advisory:` manifest lines and calls `build_manifest` on this partial dict. If `disabled_builtins` is added to the manifest, a drift-refreshed attestation will silently DROP the `disabled_builtins` line from the prior manifest, producing a cert that no longer reflects reduced coverage after a drift refresh.
findings.suggested_fix: In refresh_attestation, after reconstructing the fields dict, add: `coverage['disabled_builtins'] = manifest_disabled_builtins(prior_manifest)` so build_manifest receives and re-emits the line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the `registry_version(repo_root)` contract: what it hashes, why overlay state is included, the `stale-regver` invalidation rule, and the pattern every future gate must follow when porting `attest.py`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural contract — `registry_version()` now incorporates repo-local overlay state and will extend to code-review in story #6 — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is specified to capture this decision for future gate implementers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief CLAUDE.md entry (or `docs/adr/NNN-registry-version-contract.md`) covering: (1) `registry_version(repo_root)` hashes packaged routing + `.rebar/` overlay; (2) every gate's `compute_validity` must compare manifest regver to current regver; (3) missing regver line → stale; (4) code-review gate adopts this pattern in story #6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit implementation sequence (e.g., Step 1: overlay hashing + threading with a passing regver round-trip test; Step 2: regver staleness check in compute_validity with an invalidation test; Step 3: disabled_builtins manifest field with HMAC round-trip test) so each sub-change is independently verifiable before the next begins.
findings.finding: The plan bundles three largely independent sub-changes — (1) overlay hashing + caller threading, (2) regver staleness check in compute_validity, (3) disabled_builtins manifest field — without any sequencing guidance or thin vertical slice that de-risks the riskiest piece first. All three touch ≥3 layers (registry version computation, manifest building/parsing, validity checking, plus multiple callers) and collectively carry 9+ ACs across two AC sections.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (main) and Acceptance Criteria (remediation additions) sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the positive rationale for hashing raw overlay bytes vs. hashing only the plan_review-scoped `effective_routing(repo_root)` output, and clarify whether changes to the `code_review:` section of the overlay should or should not invalidate plan-review attestations.
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why incorporating the overlay content-hash into `registry_version()` is preferable to the equally available alternative of hashing `effective_routing(repo_root)` (the already-computed merged dict). Without a stated rationale the implementer has no design basis if the chosen approach turns out to be subtly wrong (e.g., if overlay fields outside `plan_review:` — such as a `code_review:` block — should NOT drive plan-review invalidation but ARE included in the raw overlay bytes).
findings.location: What section ('registry_version(repo_root=None) additionally hashes the .rebar/ overlay when repo_root is given')
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is plan-review scoped invalidation only, replace raw overlay hashing with `json.dumps(registry.effective_routing(repo_root), sort_keys=True)` as the overlay contribution in `registry_version(repo_root)`. If cross-gate invalidation is intentional, state that rationale explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify whether `registry_version(repo_root)` will delegate to the existing `_overlay_signature(repo_root)` in `registry.py` (promoted to a public helper or re-exported), or define a new canonical hash — and confirm a single computation path so the overlay hash in the version stamp is identical to the overlay hash used as the routing-cache key.
findings.finding: The plan proposes that `registry_version(repo_root)` 'additionally hashes the `.rebar/` overlay' but never references `_overlay_signature(repo_root)` already in `registry.py` (line 182-192), which computes exactly `sha256(overlay_bytes)` for the same file. The executor is left to independently decide whether to call `_overlay_signature()` (a private function not in the module's public API) or re-implement the hash in `attest.py`, potentially creating two divergent overlay-hash computations — a load-bearing ambiguity on the invalidation correctness contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Promote `_overlay_signature` to a public function `overlay_signature(repo_root)` in `registry.py` and have `registry_version(repo_root)` call it — one canonical overlay hash, used for both the LRU cache key and the regver stamp, so they are definitionally consistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit tests for both expand-contract paths: (a) `manifest_regver()` on a manifest with no `regver:` line returns a sentinel/None and `compute_validity` treats it as stale (not a crash), and (b) `manifest_disabled_builtins()` on a manifest with no `disabled_builtins:` line returns `[]` without raising — pair these with the existing remediation ACs.
findings.finding: The expand-contract paths — (1) an old attestation missing the `regver:` line being treated as stale (not a crash) and (2) an old attestation missing `disabled_builtins:` being parsed as `[]` (not a crash) — are explicitly flagged as safety-critical in the plan ('never a crash') but no test is specified for either. The only test specified for the regver check covers the normal invalidation path ('activating/editing a project criterion invalidates a prior attestation'), and the only test for disabled_builtins covers the round-trip on a new cert. Neither tests the 'field absent on old cert' code path that the plan's own ACs call out as a crash risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (remediation additions) — regver AC and disabled_builtins AC; main AC 'Expand-contract: an old attestation lacking the new basis is treated as stale (re-review), never a crash.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two test bullets to the remediation ACs: 'A test asserts that compute_validity treats a pre-existing attestation whose manifest has no regver: line as stale (reason stale-regver) and does not raise.' and 'A test asserts that manifest_disabled_builtins on a manifest without a disabled_builtins: line returns [] and build_manifest round-trip re-verify still passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the ordering of the new `stale-regver` check within `compute_validity`'s plan-review branch (e.g., 'checked first, before deps/head/material checks') and enumerate `'stale-regver'` explicitly in the verdict vocabulary alongside the existing values.
findings.finding: The plan's remediation section specifies adding a regver staleness check to `compute_validity`'s plan-review branch, but neither specifies the CHECK ORDERING within the branch nor defines the complete `verdict` enum value `'stale-regver'` as an entry in the existing verdict vocabulary used by callers. Tool-verified: `compute_validity` already returns `verdict` strings `'stale-reopened'`, `'stale-code'`, `'stale-head'`, `'stale-material'`, `'unverifiable-material'`, `'certified'`, `'unsigned'`, `'not-closed'`. The plan introduces `'stale-regver'` but does not specify where in the branch sequence it falls (before/after deps check), and no caller or test currently handles `'stale-regver'` — the plan's test statement only says 'a test proves activating/editing a project criterion invalidates', not that the verdict string is asserted.
findings.location: Remediation AC / compute_validity plan-review branch (attest.py lines 513-570)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly what `registry_version(repo_root)` hashes from `.rebar/` — e.g., 'the content-hash of `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` only (reusing `_overlay_signature(repo_root)`)' — and state whether `.rebar/prompts/` overrides are also included.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `registry_version(repo_root)` 'additionally hashes the `.rebar/` overlay when repo_root is given', but does not define WHAT is hashed — the single `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` file (matching the existing `_overlay_signature()` in registry.py), the full `.rebar/` directory tree, or something else. This is a load-bearing protocol decision: hashing the wrong scope either misses invalidation (directory walk catches more changes) or couples registry_version to unrelated files.
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'registry_version(repo_root) incorporates _overlay_signature(repo_root) (the sha256 of .rebar/criteria_routing.json) into its hash basis, keeping the scope identical to the overlay-cache invalidation key already used by effective_routing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'regver == today' with a precise reference: e.g., 'equals `registry_version(repo_root=None)` — i.e., the packaged-routing-only hash unchanged from before this story — when no `.rebar/` overlay is present'.
findings.finding: AC2 uses the phrase 'regver == today when overlay absent' where 'today' is ambiguous — it is unclear whether it means 'equals the pre-change packaged-routing-only hash' or 'equals whatever the current session produces'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'an unchanged overlay does not churn (regver == today when overlay absent)'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: 'an unchanged overlay does not churn: `registry_version(repo_root)` when no `.rebar/` overlay is present MUST equal `registry_version(repo_root=None)` (packaged-routing hash only).'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the plan to reflect its actual scope (e.g., 'Attestation integration: registry_version overlay hashing, disabled_builtins manifest, and regver invalidation') so the headline matches the body without a confusing 'Blocking policy' lead.
findings.finding: The title leads with 'Blocking policy' but the body explicitly disclaims blocking as out of scope ('already shipped via threshold_for + kernel Pass-3'). An implementer reading only the title will expect new blocking logic to be added, which contradicts the body's scope statement.
findings.location: Title and Scope section: 'Blocking itself is NOT in scope (already shipped via threshold_for + kernel Pass-3).'
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'Attestation integration (registry_version(repo_root), disabled_builtins, regver invalidation)' — drop 'Blocking policy' since blocking is explicitly out of scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the expand-contract/stale specification into one canonical location and clarify that ONLY a missing/mismatched `regver` line triggers stale; a missing `disabled_builtins` line is treated as `[]` (not stale) — then cross-reference rather than re-specify in each section.
findings.finding: The expand-contract / stale-on-missing-regver behavior is specified three times across different sections with slightly different framing, creating redundancy and a minor inconsistency (the What section says 'absent on old certs → treated as stale → re-review' implying ALL old certs re-review, while the remediation section says 'absent/empty on a clean run (byte-identical manifest)' which describes the no-disabled-builtins case for the `disabled_builtins` line — readers may conflate the two absent-field scenarios).
findings.location: What section ('additive/expand-contract (absent on old certs → treated as stale → re-review, never a crash)'); main AC4; Remediation preamble ('An attestation with NO regver line…'); Remediation AC1 ('a missing regver line → stale'); Remediation AC2 ('absent/empty on a clean run (byte-identical manifest)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'rebind' concretely (e.g., 'a project criterion whose `id` matches an existing built-in criterion id is rejected at overlay-load time with an explicit error') and add a corresponding test assertion to the remediation ACs.
findings.finding: AC5 states 'it can never rebind a built-in' but provides no definition of 'rebind', no enforcement mechanism, and no specified observable test — making it neither executable (E2) nor measurable (F1).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'A project criterion's verdict + coverage fold into the signed attestation; it can never rebind a built-in.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC5: 'A project criterion whose `criterion_id` collides with a built-in criterion id MUST be rejected (raise `OverlayConflictError`) at overlay-merge time (story #1 merge path); a test asserts this raises for a known built-in id. Project criteria folded into the signed attestation use only their project-namespaced ids.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete implementation description for the 'can never rebind a built-in' constraint in AC5 — e.g., specify which layer enforces it (overlay merge, manifest builder, or claim gate) and how (ID namespace separation, explicit reject, or read-only built-in table).
findings.finding: AC5 ('it can never rebind a built-in') is an acceptance criterion with no described implementation mechanism in the plan body — the plan describes how project-criterion verdicts fold into the manifest, but provides no guard, enforcement logic, or check that PREVENTS a project criterion from overwriting or rebinding a built-in's entry. This makes AC5 partially unverifiable.
findings.location: Main Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'A project criterion's verdict + coverage fold into the signed attestation; it can never rebind a built-in.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add one sentence such as: 'The overlay merge rejects any project-criterion entry whose ID matches a built-in ID (raising a configuration error), enforced in the overlay loader introduced by story #1.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
